nuggets.headline: Mastering engineer Bob Ludwig transformed 'In Rainbows' from its raw mixes
nuggets.text: The final version of *In Rainbows* you're hearing is a significant departure from the mixes producer Nigel Godrich brought to mastering. Legendary engineer Bob Ludwig revealed that his job was to realize the sound Godrich was imagining, noting that some of the records "sound nothing like the mix." According to Ludwig, every bit of distortion on the album was meticulously planned and carefully sculpted in the final mastering stage at his famously secret, intern-free Gateway Mastering studio.

source.title: Nigel Godrich and the Studio
source.quoteSnippet: Every Radiohead record, we just keep working on it until Nigel’s happy... some of these records sound nothing like the mix. You’ll be surprised at what some of the mixes sound like, but they’re all malleable. What I ended up with is what he was imagining.

nuggets.imageCaption: Mastering engineer Bob Ludwig, who shaped the final sound of 'In Rainbows'.

nuggets.headline: Radiohead first performed 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' with an orchestra in 2005
nuggets.text: This intricate, guitar-driven track began its life in a completely different form. Thom Yorke and Jonny Greenwood premiered the song at London's Ether Festival on March 27, 2005, more than two years before *In Rainbows* was released. They performed it not with the full band, but with the Nazareth Orchestra, showcasing a version built around orchestral arrangements rather than the interlocking guitar arpeggios that now define it.

source.title: Weird Fishes / Arpeggi by Radiohead - Songfacts
source.quoteSnippet: Thom Yorke and Jonny Greenwood from Radiohead together with the Nazareth Orchestra premiered this song at the Ether Festival (Royal Festival Hall, London) on March 27, 2005.

nuggets.headline: Muse producer Mark Stent worked on scrapped sessions for 'In Rainbows'
nuggets.text: Before Radiohead reunited with Nigel Godrich to create the album you know, they spent months in early 2005 working with a different producer: Mark “Spike” Stent. Those sessions were ultimately a dead end and the band scrapped the work. Stent is a key figure in the sound of some of Radiohead's most prominent contemporaries, having mixed and produced major albums for Muse, connecting the two bands through a shared, albeit abandoned, creative path.

source.title: The Making of Radiohead's 'In Rainbows'
source.quoteSnippet: The band finally got back to work in early 2005, recording on their own and later with producer Mark “Spike” Stent... But the sessions – their first in a decade with a producer other than Godrich... were a dead end.

artistSummary: Radiohead redefined alternative rock by treating the recording studio as an instrument itself. The band, fronted by Thom Yorke and guided by producer Nigel Godrich, transformed from guitar-rock heroes into experimentalists who embraced electronics, jazz, and ambient textures without losing their melodic core.
